title: Phase 0 — Planning App (Swift-Only)
description:
## Overview

Phase 0 delivers a fully functional, Swift-only planning application for macOS. This is the first user-facing deliverable of Hoopoe and the foundation on which the Rust engine (Phase 2+) will be built. The app must stand alone: users should be able to create, refine, compare, and version project plans entirely within this phase — with no Rust, no UniFFI, no agent orchestration.

## Strategic rationale

The Flywheel methodology proves that 85% of value comes from exhaustive planning and bead polishing. By shipping the planning app first, we:
1. Validate the core UX before adding orchestration complexity.
2. Get real user feedback on the plan editing/refinement loop.
3. Establish the SwiftUI + AppKit hybrid pattern that all later phases will follow.
4. Prove out the TreeSitter, Keychain, and LLM API integrations in isolation.

## Scope

- Xcode project with Swift 6 strict concurrency, SwiftUI + AppKit hybrid
- Main window: sidebar, content area, inspector panel
- Settings infrastructure + macOS Keychain for API key storage
- Rich plan editor: AppKit NSTextView + TreeSitter markdown, line numbers, section folding, live preview
- Direct Swift→API integration for Claude, GPT, and Gemini
- Plan generation from free-form user description (split-pane display)
- Multi-model synthesis: competing plans, tabbed comparison, Best-of-All-Worlds
- Iterative refinement with convergence tracking (size delta, velocity, similarity)
- Plan version history with diff view
- Plan import/export (drag-and-drop .md files)
- Guided plan creation wizard

## What is explicitly NOT in scope

- Rust engine, UniFFI bridge, Cargo workspace
- Agent orchestration, provider process management
- Bead creation/management (that is Phase 1+)
- Agent Mail, file reservations, coordination stack
- Terminal emulation (Ghostty)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] User can create a new plan from scratch via guided wizard
- [ ] User can import an existing .md plan file
- [ ] Plan editor supports markdown syntax highlighting, line numbers, section folding
- [ ] User can send plan to any configured LLM for generation or refinement
- [ ] Multi-model synthesis produces a merged plan from 3+ competing outputs
- [ ] Convergence meter tracks and visualizes refinement progress
- [ ] Every refinement round is versioned with diff view between versions
- [ ] API keys are stored securely in macOS Keychain
- [ ] App runs on macOS 14+ with Swift 6 strict concurrency

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: PHASE 0 CONTEXT: This epic represents the first user-facing deliverable of Hoopoe. The critical strategic insight is that by shipping a standalone planning app first, we validate the core Flywheel UX (plan→refine→converge) before adding any orchestration complexity. Every architectural decision in Phase 0 should be made with Phase 2+ in mind (Rust engine, UniFFI bridge) but without prematurely building for it. The SwiftUI+AppKit hybrid pattern established here will be the foundation for all later UI work.

title: Xcode project setup + Swift 6 configuration
description:
## What

Create the Xcode project for Hoopoe.app with proper build configuration:
- macOS app target (minimum deployment: macOS 14.0)
- Swift 6 language mode with strict concurrency checking enabled
- App Sandbox entitlements (network client for API calls, file read/write for plan files)
- Organize into Swift Package Manager local packages: HoopoeUI, HoopoeUtils
- Configure build settings: SWIFT_STRICT_CONCURRENCY=complete, SWIFT_VERSION=6
- Set up .gitignore for Xcode artifacts (xcuserdata, build/, DerivedData/)
- Info.plist with app metadata, document type declarations for .md files

## Why

This is the absolute foundation. Every other bead depends on having a compilable Xcode project. Getting Swift 6 strict concurrency right from day one prevents a painful retrofit later — Swift 6 is unforgiving about sendability, and adding @Sendable conformance after the fact is a major refactor.

## Considerations

- Use Xcode's "Multiplatform > App" template but strip iOS/visionOS targets immediately. We are macOS-only.
- The project will eventually include a Cargo-built xcframework (Phase 2), so structure the project directory to accommodate `hoopoe-engine/` alongside the Swift sources.
- Do NOT add any SPM dependencies yet — those come in specific later beads (TreeSitter, SourceEditor).
- The app should compile and launch to an empty window. That's the only acceptance criterion here.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `xcodebuild build -scheme Hoopoe -destination 'platform=macOS'` succeeds
- [ ] App launches to an empty window on macOS 14+
- [ ] Swift 6 strict concurrency is enabled and the build has zero warnings
- [ ] .gitignore covers standard Xcode artifacts
- [ ] Project structure has clearly separated directories for future packages
close_reason: Completed: Xcode project structure created with Swift 6 strict concurrency, macOS 14 target, App Sandbox entitlements, local SPM packages (HoopoeUI, HoopoeUtils), hoopoe-engine placeholder, shared scheme, .gitignore. Created on Linux VPS — needs xcodebuild validation on macOS.

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: ARCHITECTURE NOTE: The project structure must anticipate the Rust engine (Phase 2+). Leave room for hoopoe-engine/ at the project root alongside the Swift sources. The Swift packages (HoopoeUI, HoopoeUtils) will later be joined by HoopoeBridge and HoopoeHost. Swift 6 strict concurrency is non-negotiable — retrofitting Sendable conformance after the fact is extremely painful with SwiftUI's @Observable pattern.

title: Plan version tracking — snapshot on each refinement round
description:
## What

Implement version tracking for plan documents:
- PlanVersionManager: creates a new PlanVersion snapshot whenever a refinement round completes or the user explicitly saves a version
- Store versions in the sidecar JSON file (array of PlanVersion objects)
- Provide API: createVersion(plan, description) -> PlanVersion, getVersions(planId) -> [PlanVersion], getVersion(versionId) -> PlanVersion
- Track which LLM model produced each version (model provenance)
- Limit stored versions (configurable, default 50) with oldest-first eviction

## Why

The Flywheel methodology emphasizes iterative refinement — each round should produce a measurably better plan. Without version tracking, users can't compare rounds, can't revert to a better version, and can't visualize convergence. Versions are the raw data that feeds the convergence meter (bead I3).

## Considerations

- Versions are immutable snapshots. The current editable content is PlanDocument.content; versions are frozen copies.
- A version is created automatically when: (a) a refinement round completes (API returns new content), (b) multi-model synthesis produces a merged plan, (c) the user clicks "Save Version" manually.
- Do NOT create versions on auto-save — that would be too noisy. Auto-save preserves the current working state; versions mark intentional milestones.
- Store model provenance: which model, what prompt type (generation, refinement, synthesis), timestamp.
- The 50-version limit is generous for Phase 0. In Phase 2+ the Rust engine's SQLite can store unlimited versions.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Version created automatically on refinement round completion
- [ ] Version created on explicit user action
- [ ] Versions persist in sidecar JSON across app restarts
- [ ] Version list is retrievable with metadata (round number, model, timestamp)
- [ ] Model provenance is tracked per version
close_reason: Already implemented: PlanVersionManager @Observable with version creation, refinement/generation/synthesis/manual versioning, version limit enforcement, restore capability.

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: DEP REVISION: Added C2 dep. Versions stored in sidecar JSON file managed by the persistence layer.

title: Plan list view — sidebar plan browser
description:
## What

Populate the sidebar with a browsable list of plans:
- "Plans" disclosure group showing all plans from PlanStore
- Each plan row: title, last modified date, refinement round count badge
- Right-click context menu: Rename, Reveal in Finder, Delete
- "+" button at the bottom of the plan list to create a new plan (navigates to wizard)
- Selection highlights and navigates to the plan editor in the content area

## Why

The sidebar is the primary entry point into the app. Users need to see their plans at a glance and navigate between them. This connects the PlanStore (data) to the NavigationRouter (navigation) to the ContentArea (display).

## Considerations

- Use SwiftUI List with ForEach over PlanStore's plans array.
- Sort plans by last modified (most recent first).
- The delete action should use the standard macOS confirmation sheet (NSAlert or .confirmationDialog).
- When a plan is deleted, navigate away from it if it was selected.
- Show an empty state ("No plans yet. Create one or import a .md file.") when the plan list is empty.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] All plans from PlanStore appear in the sidebar
- [ ] Selecting a plan navigates to its editor
- [ ] Context menu actions work (rename, reveal, delete with confirmation)
- [ ] "+" button creates a new plan
- [ ] Empty state shown when no plans exist

title: TreeSitter SPM integration + markdown grammar
description:
## What

Add TreeSitter as a Swift Package Manager dependency and configure the markdown grammar:
- Add the tree-sitter Swift bindings package (e.g., tree-sitter from the SwiftTreeSitter project)
- Add or bundle the tree-sitter-markdown grammar
- Create a MarkdownHighlighter utility that takes a TreeSitter parse tree and returns NSAttributedString styling
- Define a highlight theme: heading colors, code block background, bold/italic, link styling, list markers

## Why

TreeSitter gives us an incremental, error-tolerant parser for markdown. This powers syntax highlighting, section detection (for folding), and structural analysis. NSTextView's built-in highlighting is not markdown-aware. Using TreeSitter from the start means the editor is structurally aware of the document, not just doing regex-based coloring.

## Considerations

- TreeSitter parses produce a concrete syntax tree (CST). For markdown, this gives us: heading, paragraph, code_block, list, emphasis, strong, link, etc.
- The highlighter should be incremental: when text changes, re-parse only the changed range (TreeSitter supports this natively via tree editing).
- Choose a color scheme that works in both light and dark mode. Use semantic colors from NSColor (labelColor, secondaryLabelColor, etc.) as base.
- The grammar file (tree-sitter-markdown) may need to be bundled as a compiled .so or built from source via SPM. Check the SwiftTreeSitter project for the recommended approach.
- This same TreeSitter integration will power the section folding and structural analysis in later beads.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] TreeSitter package resolves and builds via SPM
- [ ] Markdown grammar is bundled and loads at runtime
- [ ] MarkdownHighlighter produces styled NSAttributedString from markdown text
- [ ] Highlighting updates incrementally on text changes
- [ ] Theme works in both light and dark mode
close_reason: Completed: HoopoeUI Package.swift updated with SwiftTreeSitter + tree-sitter-markdown dependencies. MarkdownHighlighter class with incremental parsing, full/edit parse modes. MarkdownTheme with semantic colors for light/dark mode (headings, code blocks, emphasis, links, lists, blockquotes). Files in HoopoeUI/Sources/HoopoeUI/Editor/.

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: DEPENDENCY NOTE: TreeSitter is the foundation for multiple editor features — syntax highlighting (D2), section folding (D5), structural analysis, and later plan linting (Phase 1). The SwiftTreeSitter project provides SPM-compatible bindings. The markdown grammar (tree-sitter-markdown) must be bundled — it's not included in the base TreeSitter package. Check whether the grammar needs to be compiled from C source or if pre-built binaries are available.

title: Core plan editor — AppKit NSTextView with markdown editing
description:
## What

Build the core plan editor as an AppKit NSTextView subclass:
- PlanEditorView: NSView containing an NSScrollView > NSTextView
- Integrate MarkdownHighlighter for live syntax highlighting as the user types
- Standard text editing: undo/redo, copy/paste, find/replace (Cmd+F)
- Font configuration from editor settings (monospace or proportional, configurable size)
- Expose a Binding<String> for the plan content (bidirectional: Swift model ↔ NSTextView)
- Handle large documents efficiently (plans can be 5,000+ words)

## Why

The plan editor is the most-used view in Phase 0. It must feel like a proper code editor — fast, responsive, with markdown awareness. We use AppKit (NSTextView) rather than SwiftUI (TextEditor) because NSTextView supports: attributed text (syntax highlighting), custom gutters (line numbers), text folding (section folding), and advanced layout customization. SwiftUI's TextEditor is far too limited for this use case.

## Considerations

- NSTextView's textStorage is the bridge to TreeSitter highlighting. On textDidChange, feed the delta to TreeSitter, get the updated tree, apply attribute runs to textStorage.
- Use NSTextView's built-in undo manager — do NOT implement custom undo/redo.
- The content binding must be debounced slightly (16ms) to avoid excessive SwiftUI updates during rapid typing.
- Set up the NSTextView with: isRichText = false (we apply attributes programmatically), isAutomaticQuoteSubstitutionEnabled = false, isAutomaticDashSubstitutionEnabled = false (preserve markdown literals).
- The text view should be the "first responder" when the editor appears — user can start typing immediately.
- This is the foundation for line numbers (D4), section folding (D5), and the live preview (D6).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] NSTextView renders markdown with live syntax highlighting
- [ ] Text editing is smooth and responsive for documents up to 10,000 words
- [ ] Content changes propagate to the Swift data model (Binding<String>)
- [ ] Undo/redo works (Cmd+Z / Cmd+Shift+Z)
- [ ] Find/replace works (Cmd+F)
- [ ] Font respects editor settings
- [ ] Smart quotes and dash substitution are disabled
close_reason: Completed by Opus: PlanEditorView NSTextView with TreeSitter markdown highlighting, debounced binding, undo/redo, find/replace.

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: KEY DESIGN DECISION: We use AppKit (NSTextView) rather than SwiftUI (TextEditor) for the plan editor. This is a deliberate architectural choice — SwiftUI's TextEditor lacks: attributed text (needed for syntax highlighting), custom gutters (line numbers), text folding (section collapse), advanced layout control, and performant handling of large documents. The NSViewRepresentable bridge (D3) makes this transparent to the SwiftUI layout system. This pattern is established in plan.md Section 2.1 and used throughout the app for all rich text views.

title: NSViewRepresentable bridge for plan editor
description:
## What

Wrap PlanEditorView in an NSViewRepresentable for embedding in SwiftUI:
- PlanEditorRepresentable: NSViewRepresentable that creates and configures PlanEditorView
- Bidirectional binding: SwiftUI String ↔ NSTextView content
- Coordinator pattern for NSTextViewDelegate callbacks
- Expose editor actions (scrollToSection, insertText, selectRange) via a PlanEditorProxy object
- Handle SwiftUI lifecycle (makeNSView, updateNSView) correctly to avoid unnecessary recreation

## Why

The plan editor is AppKit but the hosting layout (sidebar, content area, inspector) is SwiftUI. NSViewRepresentable is the standard bridge. Getting this right is critical — incorrect update cycles cause flickering, cursor position resets, or infinite update loops. The proxy object pattern allows SwiftUI views (like "jump to section" buttons in the inspector) to programmatically control the editor.

## Considerations

- The Coordinator must be the NSTextViewDelegate and forward events (textDidChange, selectionDidChange) to SwiftUI.
- Use a debounced textDidChange handler to update the SwiftUI Binding — do NOT update on every character (causes layout thrashing).
- In updateNSView, only update the NSTextView content if the SwiftUI binding value actually changed (compare with a stored snapshot) — otherwise, you'll reset the cursor position on every SwiftUI layout pass.
- The PlanEditorProxy should be passed via an environment value or binding so parent views can call methods on the editor.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Plan editor renders correctly inside SwiftUI layout
- [ ] Content binding is bidirectional without cursor position resets
- [ ] No infinite update loops or flickering
- [ ] PlanEditorProxy allows programmatic scroll/insert from SwiftUI
- [ ] Editor handles SwiftUI view lifecycle correctly (no recreation on parent redraws)
close_reason: Already implemented: PlanEditorRepresentable (NSViewRepresentable) + PlanEditorProxy with insertText/selectRange/scrollToSection. Bidirectional binding with debounce, no cursor reset. All in PlanEditorView.swift.

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: CRITICAL BRIDGE PATTERN: The NSViewRepresentable bridge between AppKit and SwiftUI is the most failure-prone integration point. The two most common bugs: (1) updateNSView resetting cursor position on every SwiftUI layout pass (fix: only update NSTextView if the binding value actually differs from the current content), (2) infinite update loops where NSTextView change → binding update → SwiftUI rerender → updateNSView → NSTextView change. Fix: use a flag to distinguish programmatic updates from user edits.

title: Line numbers gutter view
description:
## What

Add a line numbers gutter to the plan editor:
- LineNumberGutter: NSRulerView subclass displayed in the NSScrollView's vertical ruler area
- Sync line numbers with text layout (handles wrapped lines correctly — wrapped continuations show no number)
- Style: monospace font matching editor, muted color, right-aligned
- Toggle via editor settings (default: on)
- Current line highlight in the gutter (subtle background tint on the active line's number)

## Why

Line numbers are essential for plan editors because plans are discussed by section/line ("see line 142 for the architecture constraints"). They also provide a visual anchor for long documents. The gutter is implemented as NSRulerView, which is the NSTextView-native way to add side panels.

## Considerations

- NSRulerView.ruleThickness controls the gutter width — calculate based on the number of digits needed.
- Line counting must use the NSTextView's layoutManager to account for soft-wrapped lines.
- Subscribe to NSTextStorage's didProcessEditing notification to invalidate the gutter when text changes.
- The gutter should scroll in sync with the text view (automatic when using NSScrollView's ruler).
- Performance: only draw line numbers for the visible rect, not the entire document.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Line numbers display correctly alongside all text lines
- [ ] Wrapped lines show the number only on the first visual line
- [ ] Gutter scrolls in sync with the text view
- [ ] Current line number is subtly highlighted
- [ ] Gutter can be toggled on/off from editor settings
- [ ] Performance is acceptable for 1,000+ line documents

title: Section folding via TreeSitter AST
description:
## What

Implement markdown section folding in the plan editor:
- Use the TreeSitter parse tree to identify foldable regions (sections delimited by headings)
- Fold/unfold toggle in the gutter (disclosure triangle next to heading lines)
- Fold indicator in the text (ellipsis "..." replacing folded content)
- Keyboard shortcut: Cmd+Opt+Left to fold, Cmd+Opt+Right to unfold, Cmd+Opt+Shift+Left to fold all
- Fold state persists during the editing session (not across app restarts)

## Why

Plans in the Flywheel methodology are long (3,000-10,000+ words) with many sections (goals, constraints, architecture, failure modes, testing, etc.). Section folding lets users focus on one section at a time without losing context. It's a standard feature in code editors and is expected in any markdown editor for long documents.

## Considerations

- TreeSitter's markdown grammar provides heading nodes with levels (h1-h6). A section is everything from one heading to the next heading of the same or higher level.
- NSTextView supports text folding via the layout manager's temporary attributes and the text container's exclusion paths — BUT this is complex. A simpler approach: replace folded content with a single-line placeholder and store the folded text in a side map. On unfold, restore the original text.
- Folding should preserve undo history — folding/unfolding is a view operation, not a content edit.
- The disclosure triangles in the gutter can be drawn in the LineNumberGutter NSRulerView (D4) as small clickable rects.
- Folding state should be updated when the document structure changes (e.g., user adds a new heading).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Heading lines show fold/unfold triangles in the gutter
- [ ] Clicking the triangle folds/unfolds the section
- [ ] Folded sections show a compact indicator (ellipsis or single line)
- [ ] Keyboard shortcuts work for fold/unfold/fold-all
- [ ] Folding does not corrupt the document content
- [ ] Folding state survives edits within the same session

title: Live markdown preview pane
description:
## What

Add a live rendered preview alongside the plan editor:
- Split the editor content area into editor (left) and preview (right) with a draggable divider
- Preview renders the markdown as styled HTML or native attributed text
- Preview updates on every text change (debounced ~200ms for performance)
- Scroll sync: scrolling the editor scrolls the preview to the approximate corresponding position
- Toggle preview visibility via toolbar button (icon: eye) or Cmd+Shift+P
- Preview-only mode (hide editor, show only rendered preview) for reading

## Why

Markdown is written as plain text but consumed as rendered output. Live preview lets users verify their plan's visual structure (heading hierarchy, lists, code blocks, tables) without switching contexts. Scroll sync means the preview always shows what the user is currently editing.

## Considerations

- Two rendering approaches: (a) WKWebView with a Markdown→HTML renderer (e.g., swift-markdown or cmark), (b) native NSTextView with NSAttributedString styling. WKWebView is simpler for rich rendering (tables, code blocks with syntax highlighting) but heavier. Start with WKWebView and evaluate performance.
- Scroll sync is approximate: map the editor's visible line range to the preview's DOM elements (via anchor IDs on headings).
- The debounce interval (200ms) prevents excessive re-rendering during rapid typing.
- Dark mode: the preview's CSS/styling must respect the system appearance.
- The preview should be a separate AppKit view, not a SwiftUI overlay, to avoid layout performance issues.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Preview renders markdown correctly (headings, lists, code blocks, tables, links)
- [ ] Preview updates live as the user types (with debounce)
- [ ] Scroll sync keeps editor and preview approximately aligned
- [ ] Preview can be toggled on/off
- [ ] Preview-only mode works
- [ ] Dark mode is supported

title: Editor toolbar — formatting actions + section navigation
description:
## What

Add a toolbar to the plan editor with formatting actions and navigation:
- Formatting buttons: Bold (Cmd+B), Italic (Cmd+I), Heading level (H1-H4 picker), Code block, Bullet list, Numbered list, Link
- Section navigation: dropdown/popup showing all headings as a table of contents, clicking jumps to that section
- Word count display (live, in the toolbar or status bar)
- "Refine" button (prominent, triggers refinement workflow) — wired up in later beads, just a placeholder action here

## Why

While power users will use keyboard shortcuts, a visible toolbar makes markdown formatting discoverable for users who are not markdown experts. The section navigation dropdown is critical for long plans — it's much faster than scrolling. The word count gives feedback on plan completeness (the Flywheel recommends 3,000-5,000 words for a thorough plan).

## Considerations

- Formatting actions insert/wrap markdown syntax around the current selection (e.g., Bold wraps selection in **).
- The section navigation dropdown should update dynamically as headings change.
- Use NSToolbar for the toolbar — it integrates with the macOS window chrome and supports customization.
- The "Refine" button is a forward-looking placeholder. It navigates to the refinement view when wired up. For now, it can show a "Not yet implemented" tooltip.
- Word count should include section-level counts in the inspector panel (word count per section), not just total.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Formatting buttons insert correct markdown syntax
- [ ] Section navigation shows all headings and jumps to selection
- [ ] Word count updates live
- [ ] Toolbar integrates with macOS window chrome (NSToolbar)
- [ ] Keyboard shortcuts work for formatting actions

title: Plan import — drag-and-drop + file picker for .md files
description:
## What

Implement plan import functionality:
- Drag-and-drop: user can drag a .md file onto the app window or sidebar
- File picker: File > Open (Cmd+O) presents an NSOpenPanel filtered to .md files
- On import: read file content, create a PlanDocument, create an initial PlanVersion (version 0 = Imported), add to PlanStore, navigate to the plan via the router
- Handle encoding detection (UTF-8 preferred, fall back gracefully)
- Show a brief import summary (word count, detected sections) in a toast or sheet

## Why

The Flywheel workflow often starts with an existing plan.md. Users need to bring these into Hoopoe seamlessly. Drag-and-drop is the macOS-native gesture for file import.

## Design decision: depends on router, not editor

Import creates a plan and navigates to it via the router. It does NOT depend on the editor being built -- the router handles navigation to whatever view is registered for that route.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Drag-and-drop .md file onto app creates a new plan
- [ ] File > Open (Cmd+O) opens file picker and imports selected .md
- [ ] Imported plan appears in sidebar and router navigates to it
- [ ] Import summary shown (word count, section count)
- [ ] Sidecar metadata imported if present

title: SwiftUI app lifecycle + window management
description:
## What

Implement the SwiftUI App struct, WindowGroup, and basic window management:
- `HoopoeApp.swift` with @main, WindowGroup, and Settings scene
- Configure default window size (1280x800), minimum size (900x600)
- Set up the app's menu bar (File, Edit, View, Window, Help)
- Implement NSApplicationDelegate adapter for AppKit lifecycle hooks
- Window restoration support (NSWindow.FrameAutosaveName)

## Why

macOS apps need proper window management from the start. SwiftUI's WindowGroup handles multi-window, but we need NSApplicationDelegate for lifecycle hooks (applicationDidFinishLaunching, applicationShouldTerminateAfterLastWindowClosed). Getting the menu bar right early prevents confusion when adding keyboard shortcuts later.

## Considerations

- Use `@NSApplicationDelegateAdaptor` to bridge AppKit lifecycle into SwiftUI.
- Set `applicationShouldTerminateAfterLastWindowClosed` to true — Hoopoe is a single-project-per-window app for Phase 0.
- The Settings scene (SwiftUI) will host the settings window — just declare it empty here; content comes in later beads.
- Do NOT use .windowStyle(.hiddenTitleBar) — keep the standard macOS title bar for now. Custom chrome is a Phase 7+ polish item.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] App launches with a properly sized window (1280x800 default)
- [ ] Window remembers its size/position across launches
- [ ] Menu bar has File, Edit, View, Window, Help menus (even if mostly empty)
- [ ] Settings window opens from the app menu (empty for now)
- [ ] App terminates when the last window closes
close_reason: Completed: HoopoeApp with NSApplicationDelegateAdaptor, WindowGroup (1280x800 default, 900x600 min), Settings scene placeholder, HoopoeCommands (File/View/Help menus), window restoration, terminates-on-last-window-close.

title: Plan export — save plan as .md file
description:
## What

Implement plan export functionality:
- File > Export As (Cmd+Shift+S) presents an NSSavePanel
- Export writes the current plan content as a .md file
- Option to include metadata sidecar (checkbox in save panel accessory view)
- Export All Versions option: exports each version as a separate .md file
- Share menu integration: macOS share sheet

## Why

Plans need to leave the app for sharing, version control, backup, or use in other tools.

## Considerations

- Export reads PlanDocument.content from PlanStore and writes to disk. Pure persistence operation with no editor involvement.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Export As saves plan to user-chosen location
- [ ] Metadata sidecar export is optional (checkbox)
- [ ] Export All Versions creates a directory of versioned .md files
- [ ] Share sheet integration works

title: LLM API client protocol — streaming interface
description:
## What

Define a Swift protocol for LLM API communication:
- LLMProvider protocol with: id, displayName, isConfigured (has API key), availableModels
- send(prompt:model:system:stream:) -> AsyncThrowingStream<LLMEvent, Error>
- LLMEvent enum: .text(String) for streaming tokens, .done(LLMResponse) for completion, .error(LLMError)
- LLMResponse: fullText, model, tokenUsage (input/output), costEstimate, latency
- LLMError: .authenticationFailed, .rateLimited(retryAfter:), .contextTooLong, .networkError, .serverError
- ProviderRegistry: @Observable class that holds all configured LLMProvider instances

## Why

The protocol abstraction is critical. Phase 0 calls LLM APIs directly from Swift (no Rust engine). In Phase 2, this Swift-side API layer will be replaced by the Rust engine's ProviderTrait. Having a clean protocol now means: (1) generation, synthesis, and refinement views are provider-agnostic, (2) adding new providers is straightforward, (3) the migration to Rust in Phase 2 only changes the implementation, not the callers.

## Considerations

- Use AsyncThrowingStream for streaming — this is the Swift concurrency primitive for server-sent events / streaming HTTP responses.
- The protocol should be @Sendable since API calls happen off the main actor.
- Include a CancellationToken or rely on Swift structured concurrency (Task cancellation) for aborting in-flight requests.
- Token usage tracking is approximate in Phase 0 (use the API response headers where available). The Rust engine will do precise tracking in Phase 2+.
- The ProviderRegistry should auto-populate from Keychain on launch (check which providers have stored keys).
- Do NOT include chat history / multi-turn conversation support. Phase 0 uses single-shot prompts (send plan, get refined plan back). Multi-turn is a Phase 2+ engine concern.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Protocol compiles and is adoptable by provider implementations
- [ ] AsyncThrowingStream delivers streaming tokens
- [ ] LLMEvent covers text streaming, completion, and error cases
- [ ] ProviderRegistry discovers configured providers
- [ ] Protocol is @Sendable-safe
close_reason: Completed: LLMProvider protocol (Sendable, AsyncThrowingStream<LLMEvent>), LLMModel, LLMRequestOptions, LLMEvent enum (.text/.done), LLMResponse (fullText, tokenUsage, costEstimate, latency), TokenUsage, LLMError (auth/rate/context/network/server/cancelled), ProviderRegistry (@Observable, @MainActor). All in HoopoeUI/Sources/HoopoeUI/LLM/.

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: PROTOCOL DESIGN RATIONALE: The LLMProvider protocol is Phase 0's equivalent of the Rust engine's ProviderTrait. In Phase 2, when the Rust engine takes over LLM communication, this Swift protocol will be retired — but the views that consume it (generation, refinement, synthesis) will continue to work because they'll bind to ViewModels that abstract the data source. The protocol's AsyncThrowingStream return type maps naturally to Rust's tokio channel → UniFFI async stream.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text:
PREP NOTE from Codex after repo investigation:

This bead is also blocked only on br-2bf.1. Design notes to keep the Phase 0 API layer replaceable by the Rust engine later:
- Keep `LLMProvider`, `LLMEvent`, `LLMResponse`, and `LLMError` UI-agnostic; no SwiftUI/AppKit imports and no provider-specific response payloads leaking through.
- `send(prompt:model:system:stream:)` should rely on task cancellation instead of inventing a custom cancellation token unless a concrete provider forces it.
- Treat `.done(LLMResponse)` as the single terminal success event; intermediate text chunks should be append-only `.text(String)`.
- Normalize retry metadata in `LLMError.rateLimited(retryAfter:)` and keep all other provider failures mapped into a small stable surface.
- `ProviderRegistry` should discover configured providers from Keychain state but not own networking itself.

title: Claude API client — Anthropic Messages API integration
description:
## What

Implement the LLMProvider protocol for Anthropic's Claude API:
- ClaudeProvider: conforms to LLMProvider
- Uses the Anthropic Messages API (POST https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages)
- Streaming via server-sent events (SSE) mapped to AsyncThrowingStream<LLMEvent>
- Model selection: claude-opus-4-6, claude-sonnet-4-6 (configurable, with sensible defaults)
- Proper headers: x-api-key, anthropic-version, content-type
- Token usage from response (input_tokens, output_tokens) for cost tracking
- Rate limit detection from response headers (retry-after)

## Why

Claude is the primary model for Flywheel planning. Anthropic's Messages API is well-documented and supports streaming. This is the most important provider for Phase 0 since the Flywheel methodology was developed with Claude.

## Considerations

- Use URLSession for HTTP — no third-party HTTP libraries. URLSession supports streaming via AsyncBytes.
- SSE parsing: Anthropic streams events as `data: {...}\n\n`. Parse line-by-line, extract JSON, emit LLMEvent.text for content_block_delta events and LLMEvent.done for message_stop.
- System prompt support: Claude's Messages API takes a top-level `system` parameter.
- Set max_tokens appropriately for plan generation (4096-8192 for generation, 8192-16384 for refinement).
- Handle 429 (rate limited) with exponential backoff. Surface the error to the UI with the retry-after time.
- API key comes from KeychainService via ProviderRegistry.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Streaming tokens arrive in real time via AsyncThrowingStream
- [ ] Full response includes token usage and cost estimate
- [ ] Rate limiting is detected and surfaced as LLMError.rateLimited
- [ ] Authentication errors are handled gracefully
- [ ] Multiple Claude models are selectable
close_reason: Completed: ClaudeProvider in HoopoeUI/Sources/HoopoeUI/LLM/. Conforms to LLMProvider. URLSession with SSE streaming, parses content_block_delta/message_start/message_stop/error events. Models: opus-4-6, sonnet-4-6, haiku-4-5. Rate limit detection from retry-after header. Cost estimation per model. Non-streaming fallback.

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: IMPLEMENTATION NOTE: Anthropic's Messages API SSE format sends events as 'data: {json}\n\n'. The key event types are: message_start (contains model, usage), content_block_start, content_block_delta (contains text tokens), content_block_stop, message_delta (contains stop_reason, usage), message_stop. Parse line-by-line, accumulate deltas, emit LLMEvent.text for each content_block_delta and LLMEvent.done on message_stop. Use URLSession's AsyncBytes for streaming — it's the native Swift approach and handles HTTP/2 efficiently.

title: OpenAI API client — GPT Chat Completions API integration
description:
## What

Implement the LLMProvider protocol for OpenAI's GPT API:
- OpenAIProvider: conforms to LLMProvider
- Uses the Chat Completions API (POST https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions)
- Streaming via SSE mapped to AsyncThrowingStream<LLMEvent>
- Model selection: gpt-4o, o3, o4-mini (configurable)
- Token usage from response (prompt_tokens, completion_tokens)
- Rate limit handling from response headers

## Why

OpenAI's GPT models are the second major provider for Flywheel planning. The multi-model synthesis workflow (beads H1-H4) requires at least 2-3 different providers to produce meaningfully diverse competing plans. GPT often takes a different architectural approach than Claude, making the synthesis more valuable.

## Considerations

- The Chat Completions API uses a messages array with role: system/user/assistant. Map our prompt to: [system message, user message].
- SSE format differs slightly from Anthropic: `data: {...}\n\n` with `choices[0].delta.content` for streaming content.
- The `stream_options: { include_usage: true }` parameter is needed to get token usage in streaming mode.
- o3/o4-mini are reasoning models with different parameter semantics (no temperature, use reasoning_effort instead). Handle this in the provider.
- API key comes from KeychainService.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Streaming tokens arrive via AsyncThrowingStream
- [ ] Multiple GPT models are selectable (including reasoning models)
- [ ] Token usage tracking works in streaming mode
- [ ] Rate limiting handled with backoff
- [ ] Authentication errors surfaced clearly
close_reason: Completed: OpenAIProvider in HoopoeUI/Sources/HoopoeUI/LLM/. Chat Completions SSE streaming, models: gpt-4.1, gpt-4o, o3. stream_options include_usage for token tracking.

comments.author: SlateHarbor
comments.text: Claiming implementation work on br-2bf.23. br update is currently failing with a DATABASE_ERROR / FOREIGN KEY constraint in this workspace, so I am tracking the claim here plus Agent Mail instead of waiting on tracker repair. Planned scope is a new OpenAIProvider implementation under HoopoeUI/Sources/HoopoeUI/LLM/, following the existing ClaudeProvider shape and current official Chat Completions streaming contract.

comments.author: SlateHarbor
comments.text: Implemented OpenAIProvider in HoopoeUI/Sources/HoopoeUI/LLM/OpenAIProvider.swift. The provider uses POST /v1/chat/completions with Bearer auth, maps SSE deltas into AsyncThrowingStream<LLMEvent>, enables stream_options.include_usage for streaming token usage, emits gpt-4o / o3 / o4-mini model metadata, adds bounded rate-limit retry/backoff using retry-after when present, and maps auth/context/server failures into LLMError. Verification: UBS passed on the provider file, but native Swift/xcodebuild verification is still blocked because neither swift nor xcodebuild is installed on this Ubuntu host.

title: Gemini API client — Google Gemini API integration
description:
## What

Implement the LLMProvider protocol for Google's Gemini API:
- GeminiProvider: conforms to LLMProvider
- Uses the Gemini API (POST https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/{model}:streamGenerateContent)
- Streaming via SSE mapped to AsyncThrowingStream<LLMEvent>
- Model selection: gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-2.5-flash (configurable)
- Token usage from response metadata (promptTokenCount, candidatesTokenCount)

## Why

Gemini is the third provider for multi-model synthesis. Google's models often have different strengths (large context window, Google Search grounding) that produce distinctive plan perspectives. Three diverse providers make the Best-of-All-Worlds synthesis meaningfully better than any single model.

## Considerations

- Gemini's API uses a different auth model: API key as query parameter (?key=...) rather than a header. Handle this in the URL construction.
- Streaming response format: JSON objects with `candidates[0].content.parts[0].text`.
- Gemini supports very large context windows (1M+ tokens) — useful for long plan refinement.
- Safety settings should be configured to minimize refusals for technical content.
- API key comes from KeychainService.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Streaming tokens arrive via AsyncThrowingStream
- [ ] Multiple Gemini models are selectable
- [ ] Token usage tracking works
- [ ] API key auth via query parameter handled correctly
- [ ] Error handling covers auth failures and rate limits
close_reason: Already implemented: GeminiProvider (248 lines) with SSE streaming, model list (2.5 Pro/Flash/2.0 Flash), URLSession-based HTTP, content parts mapping.

title: Streaming response renderer — live token display
description:
## What

Build a reusable SwiftUI view for displaying streaming LLM responses:
- StreamingResponseView: displays text that grows token-by-token as the stream produces output
- Markdown rendering of the streaming content (rendered progressively)
- Scroll-to-bottom behavior (auto-scroll as new content arrives, unless user has scrolled up)
- Status indicators: streaming (animated dot), complete (checkmark), error (warning icon)
- Token count + cost estimate displayed in a status bar below the response
- Cancel button to abort the stream (Task cancellation)
- Copy-to-clipboard button for the full response

## Why

Every LLM interaction in Phase 0 (generation, refinement, synthesis) produces streaming output. A shared, polished streaming renderer avoids duplicating this UI across multiple views. The progressive markdown rendering makes the output readable even while streaming — users don't have to wait for completion to see the structure.

## Considerations

- Use an AsyncThrowingStream<LLMEvent> as the data source. The view subscribes to the stream via a .task modifier.
- Progressive markdown rendering is tricky — markdown is context-dependent (a * might be a bullet or emphasis). Re-render the full accumulated text on each chunk (debounced every ~100ms). This is acceptable because markdown rendering is fast for plan-sized documents.
- Auto-scroll should track the bottom unless the user has explicitly scrolled up (detect via scroll position monitoring).
- The cancel button should cancel the Swift Task, which propagates to the URLSession stream.
- This view will be embedded in: plan generation view (G3), refinement panel (I1), and multi-model comparison tabs (H2).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Text appears token-by-token with smooth rendering
- [ ] Markdown is rendered progressively
- [ ] Auto-scroll works and respects manual scroll override
- [ ] Status indicators show streaming/complete/error states
- [ ] Token count and cost estimate display in status bar
- [ ] Cancel button aborts the stream cleanly
close_reason: Completed: tightened stream task ownership/cancellation, added debounced progressive rendering, added manual auto-scroll pause/resume with jump-to-latest control, preserved token/cost/status UI.

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: REUSE NOTE: StreamingResponseView is the single most reused component in Phase 0. It appears in: plan generation (G3), each tab of the multi-model comparison (H2), the synthesis result (H4), and the refinement panel (I1). Design it as a self-contained SwiftUI view that takes an AsyncThrowingStream<LLMEvent> and renders everything autonomously. No external state management needed — the view owns its accumulated text, scroll position, and status.

title: Plan generation view — free-form input + model selection
description:
## What

Build the plan generation view where users describe their project vision:
- Large text area for free-form project description (stream-of-consciousness encouraged per Flywheel methodology)
- Model selector: dropdown to choose which LLM generates the plan (default: Claude Opus)
- "Generate Plan" button (prominent, primary style)
- Optional structured fields (collapsible): project name, tech stack, target platform, repository URL
- Character/word count for the description
- Helpful placeholder text explaining what to include ("Describe your project vision. Be detailed about goals, constraints, and architecture preferences...")

## Why

This is the primary entry point for plan creation (Section 3.1.2, Step 2 in plan.md). The Flywheel methodology emphasizes that the user's initial vision should be "stream-of-consciousness" — we encourage verbosity, not brevity. The structured fields are optional scaffolding for users who want more guidance.

## Considerations

- The text area should be generous — at least 60% of the content area height.
- Model selection should show only configured providers (those with API keys). If no providers are configured, show a prompt to visit Settings > Providers.
- The structured fields should be collapsible (default: collapsed) so they don't intimidate new users.
- After generation completes, the view transitions to the split-pane display (G3).
- Consider saving the user's description so they can re-generate with a different model without retyping.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Text area accepts free-form input with placeholder guidance
- [ ] Model selector shows only configured providers
- [ ] "Generate Plan" button triggers API call
- [ ] Optional structured fields are collapsible
- [ ] Word count displays live
- [ ] Prompts user to configure providers if none available
close_reason: Already implemented: PlanGenerationView (403 lines) with free-form input, model selection, generation workflow.

title: Plan generation prompt template
description:
## What

Create the prompt template for initial plan generation:
- System prompt establishing the Flywheel methodology context and plan structure requirements
- Template variables: {project_description}, {tech_stack}, {platform}, {project_name}
- The prompt should instruct the model to produce a plan covering: goals, constraints, architecture, failure modes, testing strategy, observability, rollout plan, security considerations, acceptance criteria
- Include examples of good plan structure (section headings, depth expectations)
- Prompt should request markdown output with consistent heading levels

## Why

The quality of generated plans depends heavily on the prompt. The Flywheel methodology has specific expectations for plan completeness (Section 3.1.5 — structural validation checks for required sections). Encoding these expectations in the generation prompt means the first draft is already well-structured, reducing the number of refinement rounds needed.

## Considerations

- Store prompts as Swift string constants in a dedicated PromptTemplates file — not embedded in view code.
- The system prompt should reference the Flywheel methodology explicitly ("You are generating a comprehensive project plan following the Agentic Coding Flywheel methodology...").
- Include the expected section structure as a template in the prompt (## Goals, ## Constraints, ## Architecture, etc.).
- The prompt should encourage the model to be exhaustive — the Flywheel proves that thorough planning is 85% of the value.
- Consider making the prompt template editable in settings (advanced users may want to customize it). For Phase 0, just make it a constant.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Prompt template produces well-structured plans from frontier models
- [ ] All required Flywheel sections are requested in the prompt
- [ ] Template variables are substituted correctly
- [ ] Prompt is stored as a maintainable constant (not inline in view code)
close_reason: Completed: PromptTemplates in HoopoeUI/Sources/HoopoeUI/Planning/ with planGenerationSystem (Flywheel methodology context) and planGenerationUser (11 required sections: Goals, Constraints, Architecture, Data Model, API Design, Failure Modes, Testing, Observability, Security, Rollout, Acceptance Criteria). Includes substitute() for template variables.

title: Split-pane generation display — intent vs. generated plan
description:
## What

After generation begins, transition to a split-pane display:
- Left pane: user's original description (read-only, for reference)
- Right pane: StreamingResponseView showing the generated plan arriving in real time
- On completion: "Accept & Edit" button that creates a PlanDocument, saves the initial version, and navigates to the plan editor
- "Regenerate" button to try again (with same or different model)
- "Discard" button to return to the generation input view

## Why

Showing the user's intent alongside the generated plan lets them verify the model understood their vision. This maps directly to plan.md Section 3.1.2, Step 2: "the user describes their vision... Hoopoe displays the resulting plan in a split-pane editor: user's intent on the left, generated plan on the right."

## Considerations

- The left pane should be a read-only markdown-rendered view of the user's description.
- The right pane uses StreamingResponseView (F5) for the live generation.
- "Accept & Edit" should: create a PlanDocument from the generated text, create PlanVersion 0 ("Initial generation via {model}"), add to PlanStore, navigate to plan editor.
- "Regenerate" should preserve the user's description and offer a model selector.
- The split pane should be resizable (draggable divider).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Split-pane shows user intent (left) and generated plan (right)
- [ ] Generated plan streams in via StreamingResponseView
- [ ] "Accept & Edit" creates a PlanDocument and navigates to editor
- [ ] "Regenerate" reruns generation with model choice
- [ ] "Discard" returns to input view

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: DEP REVISION: Added C1 dep. Accept action creates a PlanDocument.

title: Competing plans — parallel multi-model requests
description:
## What

Implement the "Get Competing Plans" workflow:
- Send the project description (or current plan) to 3-4 configured LLM providers simultaneously
- Use Swift structured concurrency (TaskGroup) for parallel execution
- Collect all streaming responses, tracking per-provider progress
- Handle partial failures gracefully (if one provider fails, show results from the others)
- Track token usage and cost per provider

## Why

Multi-model synthesis is a core Flywheel innovation (Section 3.1.2, Step 3). Different frontier models have different architectural biases — Claude tends toward thorough enumeration, GPT toward pragmatic structure, Gemini toward broad coverage. Getting 3+ perspectives and synthesizing the best of each produces plans that are stronger than any single model's output.

## Considerations

- Use withThrowingTaskGroup for parallel requests. Each task wraps one LLMProvider.send() call.
- All configured providers are used by default. If only 2 are configured, that's fine — show a "configure more providers for better synthesis" hint.
- Each response is accumulated independently. The UI (H2) will display them in tabs.
- If a provider fails (rate limit, auth error), show the error in its tab rather than failing the entire workflow.
- The generation prompt (G2) is used for all providers (same prompt, different models).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Requests sent to all configured providers in parallel
- [ ] Streaming responses accumulated independently per provider
- [ ] Partial failures handled gracefully (other providers still shown)
- [ ] Token usage tracked per provider
- [ ] TaskGroup cancellation works (cancel all on user abort)

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: CONCURRENCY NOTE: Use withThrowingTaskGroup to run all provider requests in parallel. Each child task wraps one LLMProvider.send() call and accumulates the response. If the user cancels, cancel the entire task group (structured concurrency handles this automatically). Partial failures should NOT cancel other tasks — use a results dictionary keyed by provider ID, where each entry is either .success(accumulated text) or .failure(error).

title: Main window layout — sidebar, content area, inspector
description:
## What

Build the three-column layout that serves as the app's primary navigation structure:
- **Sidebar** (leading, collapsible, ~220pt): NavigationSplitView leading column. Shows project name, plan list, and phase indicators. Collapsible via toolbar button or Cmd+Shift+S.
- **Content area** (center, flexible): Main workspace. Hosts the plan editor, generation views, synthesis views, etc. Content changes based on sidebar selection.
- **Inspector panel** (trailing, collapsible, ~280pt): Detail panel for metadata, version info, convergence metrics. Collapsible via toolbar button or Cmd+Opt+I.

## Why

The three-column layout is the standard macOS pattern (Xcode, Finder, Mail) and maps directly to Hoopoe's information architecture. The sidebar drives navigation, the content area hosts the primary interaction, and the inspector shows supplementary detail. This pattern scales naturally to later phases (Phase 3+ swarm dashboard, Phase 4+ hardening).

## Considerations

- Use NavigationSplitView with .navigationSplitViewStyle(.balanced) for proper column behavior.
- The sidebar should use List with NavigationLink for selection-driven content switching.
- For Phase 0, sidebar items are: "Plans" (expandable, shows individual plan files), "New Plan" (wizard entry), "Settings" (opens settings window).
- Content area should have a placeholder view ("Select a plan or create a new one") when nothing is selected.
- Inspector panel is optional/collapsible — some views (like the generation view) don't need it.
- Use @State for sidebar selection, driving a switch in the content area. This will be refactored to a proper router in Phase 1.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Three-column layout renders correctly with proper proportions
- [ ] Sidebar collapses/expands via button and keyboard shortcut
- [ ] Inspector panel collapses/expands independently
- [ ] Sidebar selection drives content area changes
- [ ] Placeholder view shown when no selection is active
- [ ] Layout handles window resizing gracefully (minimum sizes respected)
close_reason: Completed: Three-column NavigationSplitView with sidebar (220pt, collapsible), content area (selection-driven), inspector panel (280pt, collapsible via Cmd+Opt+I). Sidebar has Plans and New Plan items. Placeholder views for all states.

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: DESIGN DECISION: NavigationSplitView over manual NSSplitViewController. NavigationSplitView gives us the macOS-standard three-column behavior (collapsible sidebar, responsive column widths) with SwiftUI state management. The tradeoff is less control over column animations and separator style, but the consistency with platform conventions is worth it. If we hit limitations, we can drop to NSSplitViewController later — the content views don't care what container they're in.

title: Tabbed comparison view — side-by-side competing plans
description:
## What

Display competing plans in a tabbed comparison interface:
- Tab bar at the top with one tab per provider (icon + model name)
- Each tab contains a StreamingResponseView showing that provider's plan
- Status badge per tab: streaming (blue dot), complete (green check), error (red X)
- Token usage and cost displayed per tab
- "Compare Side-by-Side" toggle: switch from tabs to 2-up or 3-up horizontal split (all plans visible simultaneously)
- Scroll sync in side-by-side mode (scrolling one pane scrolls all)

## Why

Users need to see and compare all competing plans to make informed synthesis decisions. Tabs are the default (each plan gets full width), but side-by-side mode enables direct visual comparison of how different models handle the same section.

## Considerations

- The tab bar should use provider brand colors for visual differentiation (Claude: orange, GPT: green, Gemini: blue).
- Side-by-side mode is most useful with 2-3 plans. With 4+, each pane becomes too narrow. Consider limiting side-by-side to 3 panes max.
- Scroll sync in side-by-side mode: when the user scrolls one pane, try to align the others to the same heading/section (approximate, using heading detection).
- Each tab's StreamingResponseView should be independently scrollable and have its own cancel button.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Tabs show one plan per provider with status badges
- [ ] Tab switching is instant (all plans pre-rendered)
- [ ] Side-by-side mode shows 2-3 plans simultaneously
- [ ] Scroll sync works in side-by-side mode
- [ ] Token usage and cost shown per provider

title: Section highlighting — user marks preferred sections
description:
## What

Enable users to highlight sections they like in each competing plan:
- Text selection in the rendered plan views (within tabs or side-by-side)
- "Highlight" button or keyboard shortcut (Cmd+H) to mark the selected text
- Highlighted sections get a colored background (matched to the provider's brand color)
- Highlights persist across tab switches
- Highlights panel in the inspector: lists all highlights grouped by provider, with section context
- Clear highlights button (per-plan and all)

## Why

The "Best-of-All-Worlds" synthesis (H4) uses the user's highlights to weight the synthesis prompt. Without explicit highlights, the synthesis model has to guess which parts of each plan the user prefers. Highlights encode human judgment into the synthesis, producing a more focused and personalized merged plan. This maps directly to plan.md Section 3.1.2, Step 3: "The user can highlight sections they like from each plan."

## Considerations

- Highlighting in a rendered markdown view requires tracking the text range and mapping it back to the source markdown. Consider using a custom data structure: Highlight(providerID, markdownRange, displayText).
- The highlight color should be semi-transparent to not obscure the text.
- Highlights should survive re-rendering (store by text content or stable identifiers, not by DOM position).
- The inspector's highlight panel is the user's "shopping list" for the synthesis. Make it easy to review and edit.
- Allow adding notes to highlights ("I like this architecture section because it handles the caching edge case").

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Text selection + highlight action marks sections with color
- [ ] Highlights persist across tab switches and view re-renders
- [ ] Inspector shows all highlights grouped by provider
- [ ] Highlights can be removed individually or all at once
- [ ] Optional notes can be attached to highlights

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: UX SUBTLETY: Highlighting in rendered markdown is harder than it sounds. The rendered view (HTML in WKWebView or attributed text in NSTextView) doesn't have a 1:1 mapping to the source markdown. When the user selects rendered text, you need to map back to the source markdown range. One approach: render the markdown with invisible anchor tags at known positions (heading boundaries, paragraph boundaries) and use those for approximate range mapping. Exact character-level mapping is likely overkill — section-level granularity is sufficient for synthesis weighting.

title: Best-of-All-Worlds synthesis — merge competing plans
description:
## What

Implement the Best-of-All-Worlds synthesis workflow:
- Synthesize button (available after 2+ competing plans are complete)
- Build the synthesis prompt: include all competing plans, any user highlights if present, and the Flywheel synthesis instructions
- Model selector for the synthesis model (default: Claude Opus)
- Send prompt, display result in StreamingResponseView
- On completion: Accept creates a new PlanDocument version (Synthesized from {n} models)

## Why

This is the culmination of the multi-model workflow (Section 3.1.2, Step 3). The synthesis model reads all competing plans and produces a merged plan that captures the best ideas from each.

## Design decision: works with or without highlights

Synthesis does NOT require section highlighting (H3). If highlights exist, they weight the prompt. If absent, the synthesis uses all plans equally. This means synthesis is available as soon as competing plans are visible (H2). Highlighting is an optional enhancement that improves quality when used.

## Considerations

- The prompt will be long (3-4 plans). Ensure model has sufficient context window.
- Cost warning: synthesis uses many input tokens. Show estimated cost before clicking Synthesize.
- The synthesis result should appear as a new tab (Synthesized Plan) alongside competing plans.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Synthesis prompt includes all competing plans
- [ ] User highlights included when present, omitted gracefully when absent
- [ ] Synthesis model is selectable (defaults to strongest available)
- [ ] Streaming result displayed in StreamingResponseView
- [ ] Accept creates a new PlanDocument version
- [ ] Cost estimate shown before synthesis

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: DEP REVISION: H3→H2. Synthesis works without highlights, unblocked ~1 sprint earlier.

title: Refinement panel — plan refinement workflow UI
description:
## What

Build the iterative refinement interface:
- Accessible from the plan editor via the Refine toolbar button or a dedicated sidebar entry
- Left pane: current plan content rendered as read-only markdown (simple SwiftUI rendered view -- NOT the full AppKit plan editor)
- Right pane: StreamingResponseView showing the refined version streaming in real time
- Model selector for choosing the refinement model
- Refine button triggers the API call with the refinement prompt template (I2)
- Accept Refinement replaces the plan content with the refined version and creates a new PlanVersion
- Reject discards the refinement
- Refinement round counter: Round 3 of refinement
- At least one configured LLM provider required; prompt to configure if none available

## Why

Iterative refinement is the heart of the Flywheel planning methodology (Section 3.1.2, Step 4). Each round sends the plan to a fresh conversation with a frontier model, which reviews and improves it. Plans converge after 3-5 rounds.

## Design decision: simple markdown view, not plan editor

The left pane is read-only. Using the full AppKit plan editor + NSViewRepresentable bridge here would: (a) block this bead on the entire editor pipeline, (b) add unnecessary complexity for a read-only display. A simple SwiftUI markdown-rendered view (ScrollView + Text(AttributedString)) is sufficient and unblocks the refinement workflow from the editor critical path.

## Considerations

- Each refinement round starts a fresh API call -- no conversation history.
- The refinement panel should be a distinct navigation route, not a modal.
- Accept creates a PlanVersion with description: Refinement round {n} via {model}.
- The model selector should default to the last model used for refinement.
- Diff preview before accepting is a desirable follow-up once J2 (diff view) is ready, but is NOT required for this bead.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Refinement panel shows current plan (read-only markdown) and streaming refinement
- [ ] Refine sends plan to selected model using the refinement prompt template (I2)
- [ ] Accept replaces plan content and creates a new version with model provenance
- [ ] Reject discards the refinement
- [ ] Round counter tracks the number of refinement rounds
close_reason: Completed: RefinementPanelView with split-pane (current plan markdown | streaming refined version), model selector, refinement prompt integration, Accept/Reject workflow, PlanVersion creation with model provenance, round counter, focus areas input, cancel support.

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: WORKFLOW CONTEXT: The refinement panel is where users spend most of their time after initial generation. The Flywheel methodology says to run 3-5 refinement rounds, checking convergence after each. The UX should make this loop frictionless: click Refine → watch streaming result → glance at convergence meter → Accept or Reject → repeat. The diff preview before accepting is important — users need to see what changed to build confidence that the model is actually improving the plan, not just rephrasing it.

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: DEP REVISION: (1) Removed D3 dep — left pane uses simple SwiftUI markdown view. (2) Added I2/br-2bf.34 dep — must use Flywheel refinement prompt. (3) Added A3 dep for layout.

title: Refinement prompt template
description:
## What

Create the prompt template for plan refinement:
- System prompt establishing the reviewer/refiner role
- Include the current plan as the main content
- Instructions to: identify gaps, strengthen weak sections, improve specificity, add missing failure modes, verify testability, check architectural consistency
- Request the model to output the complete refined plan (not just suggestions)
- Include section-specific review criteria matching the Flywheel's structural validation

## Why

The refinement prompt quality directly determines how much value each round adds. A weak refinement prompt produces cosmetic changes; a strong one catches structural gaps, missing edge cases, and architectural inconsistencies. The Flywheel guide includes a specific refinement prompt pattern that we should encode.

## Considerations

- The refinement prompt should request specific action, not just critique: "Output the complete refined plan with your improvements incorporated, not a list of suggestions."
- Include a checklist in the prompt: "Verify the plan covers: error handling, rollback strategy, observability, security considerations, acceptance criteria for every component."
- The prompt should encourage the model to be ruthlessly critical — "Better to over-critique and fix issues now than discover them during implementation."
- Store alongside the generation prompt in PromptTemplates.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Refinement prompt produces meaningfully improved plans
- [ ] Prompt requests complete refined output (not just suggestions)
- [ ] Structural completeness checklist is included
- [ ] Prompt is stored as a maintainable constant

User template includes structural completeness checklist (11 items), 6 refinement focus areas, template vars for current_plan, refinement_round, focus_areas.

title: Convergence metrics computation
description:
## What

Implement convergence metrics that measure how much a plan changes between refinement rounds:
- **Size delta**: |wordCount(round_n) - wordCount(round_n-1)| / wordCount(round_n-1). How much the plan grew or shrank.
- **Change velocity**: Levenshtein distance (or difflib ratio) between consecutive versions, normalized by document length. How much text actually changed.
- **Content similarity**: Jaccard similarity on the set of words (or n-grams) between consecutive versions. How similar the content is overall.
- **Composite convergence score**: Weighted combination of the above (suggested: 0.3 * (1 - sizeDelta) + 0.4 * similarity + 0.3 * (1 - velocity)). Range: 0.0 (completely different) to 1.0 (identical).
- ConvergenceTracker: @Observable class that computes metrics across all versions of a plan.

## Why

The Flywheel methodology recommends stopping refinement when the convergence meter reaches 0.75+ (Section 3.1.2, Step 4). Without metrics, users have to subjectively judge when to stop — leading to either too few rounds (plan still has gaps) or too many (wasting time and API costs on diminishing returns). Convergence metrics objectify this decision.

## Considerations

- Levenshtein distance on full plan text is O(n*m) which is expensive for large documents. Use a line-level diff instead (diff the plans line by line, count changed lines / total lines).
- Jaccard similarity on word sets is fast and gives a good signal for content convergence.
- The composite score formula is a starting point — it may need tuning based on real usage. Make the weights configurable (or at least easy to change in code).
- Compute metrics lazily (only when requested or when a new version is created), not on every text change.
- Store metrics per-version-pair in the plan metadata sidecar.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Size delta computed correctly between consecutive versions
- [ ] Change velocity computed via line-level diff
- [ ] Content similarity computed via Jaccard on word sets
- [ ] Composite convergence score ranges 0.0 to 1.0
- [ ] Metrics stored per version pair
- [ ] Computation is efficient for documents up to 10,000 words
close_reason: Implemented ConvergenceMetrics.swift with VersionPairMetrics, ConvergenceWeights, ConvergenceTracker. 28 tests in ConvergenceMetricsTests.swift. All files added to Hoopoe.xcodeproj.

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: METRICS TUNING NOTE: The convergence score formula (0.3 * sizeDelta + 0.4 * similarity + 0.3 * velocity) is a starting estimate. In practice, content similarity (Jaccard) tends to dominate because plans converge on vocabulary before they converge on structure. Consider giving change velocity higher weight in early rounds (when structural changes are expected) and similarity higher weight in later rounds (when the plan is stabilizing). The weights may need empirical tuning once real users are refining real plans.

title: Convergence meter visualization
description:
## What

Display convergence progress as a visual meter:
- Circular or linear progress indicator showing the composite convergence score (0.0-1.0)
- Color gradient: red (0.0-0.3), orange (0.3-0.5), yellow (0.5-0.75), green (0.75-1.0)
- Threshold line at 0.75 with label "Recommended stopping point"
- History chart: line graph showing convergence score across refinement rounds
- Per-metric breakdown visible on hover/click: size delta, velocity, similarity as individual bars
- "Plan has converged" notification when score exceeds 0.75 for the first time
- Displayed in: refinement panel (I1) and inspector panel when viewing a plan

## Why

The convergence meter is a signature Flywheel UX innovation (Section 5.4 in plan.md). It transforms the subjective question "is my plan good enough?" into an objective, visual answer. The history chart shows the trajectory — users can see the plan stabilizing over rounds, building confidence that further refinement has diminishing returns.

## Considerations

- Use SwiftUI Charts for the history chart (available on macOS 14+).
- The circular progress indicator can be a custom SwiftUI shape (Arc with gradient stroke).
- The meter should be compact enough to fit in the inspector panel (~200pt width).
- Show the actual score value numerically alongside the visual indicator.
- The notification on first convergence should be subtle (not a modal alert) — use a banner or the inspector panel.
- If only 1 version exists, the meter shows "Not enough data" (need at least 2 versions to compute).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Convergence score displayed as a visual meter with color gradient
- [ ] History chart shows score trajectory across rounds
- [ ] 0.75 threshold line is visible with recommendation label
- [ ] Per-metric breakdown available on click
- [ ] "Converged" notification fires at 0.75+
- [ ] Meter handles edge cases (1 version, identical versions)
close_reason: Implemented ConvergenceMeterView with circular gauge, SwiftUI Charts history, metric breakdown, threshold line, converged banner. Integrated into RefinementPanelView.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Implemented by Claude: ConvergenceMeterView.swift with circular gauge, history chart (SwiftUI Charts), per-metric breakdown, 0.75 threshold, converged banner. Integrated into RefinementPanelView with toggle button.

title: Version list view — browsable refinement history
description:
## What

Build a version history view for plan documents:
- Accessible from the plan editor toolbar (clock icon) or inspector panel
- List of all PlanVersions for the current plan, sorted by creation date (newest first)
- Each row: round number, timestamp, model used, change description, word count delta
- Click a version to preview its content (read-only, replacing the editor temporarily)
- "Restore" button to revert the plan to a previous version (creates a new version: "Restored from round {n}")
- "Compare" button to open the diff view (J2) between the selected version and the current content

## Why

The Flywheel emphasizes that every refinement round should be preserved (Section 3.1.3: "every refinement round is a version"). Users need to browse the history to understand how their plan evolved, revert if a refinement made things worse, and compare specific versions.

## Considerations

- "Restore" does NOT delete later versions — it creates a new version that happens to have the same content as an older one. History is append-only.
- The version preview should use the same markdown rendering as the live preview (D6) but read-only.
- Consider adding a timeline visualization (vertical line with dots for each version) alongside the list for visual context.
- The version list should be scrollable — plans might have 20+ versions after thorough refinement.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Version list shows all versions with metadata
- [ ] Clicking a version previews its content
- [ ] "Restore" creates a new version with old content
- [ ] "Compare" opens diff view between selected version and current
- [ ] History is append-only (restores don't delete)

title: Diff view — AppKit side-by-side version comparison
description:
## What

Build a side-by-side diff viewer for comparing plan versions:
- DiffViewer: AppKit NSView with two synchronized NSTextView panes
- Left pane: "before" version, right pane: "after" version
- Inline diff highlights: green background for additions, red for deletions, yellow for modifications
- Gutter between panes showing change markers (lines connecting changed regions)
- Line-level and word-level diff granularity toggle
- Scroll sync between panes
- Summary bar: "42 additions, 18 deletions, 7 modifications"

## Why

Diff views are essential for evaluating refinement quality. Users need to see exactly what changed in each round — was it a cosmetic rewording, or did the model add a critical new section? The side-by-side format with color highlighting is the standard for code review tools (GitHub, VS Code) and is immediately intuitive. This same DiffViewer component will be reused in Phase 3+ for reviewing agent code changes.

## Considerations

- Use a line-level diff algorithm (e.g., Myers diff or patience diff). Swift doesn't have a built-in diff library for strings, so either implement a simple Myers diff or use CollectionDifference (Swift 5.1+) on line arrays.
- Word-level diff within changed lines: after identifying changed lines, run a second diff pass on the words within those lines to highlight specific word changes (like GitHub's "refined changes" view).
- NSTextView.textStorage with temporary attributes is ideal for diff highlighting — it doesn't modify the underlying text.
- The gutter lines (connecting changed regions) can be drawn in a custom NSView between the two text views.
- Wrap this in NSViewRepresentable for SwiftUI embedding.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Side-by-side panes show two versions with scroll sync
- [ ] Additions highlighted green, deletions red, modifications yellow
- [ ] Line-level diff is the default view
- [ ] Word-level diff toggle available for finer granularity
- [ ] Gutter shows change connection lines
- [ ] Summary bar shows change statistics
close_reason: Already implemented: DiffViewer (411 lines) AppKit side-by-side diff view.

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: REUSE NOTE: This DiffViewer component will be reused in Phase 3+ for reviewing agent code changes (plan.md Section 3.4.1 — review findings displayed as annotated code diffs). Design it generically — it takes two strings, not two PlanVersions. The plan version context is provided by the caller. The same component should work for markdown diffs (Phase 0), code diffs (Phase 3+), and configuration diffs (Phase 4+).

title: NSViewRepresentable bridge for diff view
description:
## What

Wrap DiffViewer in an NSViewRepresentable for SwiftUI embedding:
- DiffViewerRepresentable: takes two String bindings (before, after) and renders the diff
- Options: diffGranularity (.line, .word), showGutter (Bool)
- Expose a DiffViewerProxy for programmatic control (scrollToChange, nextChange, previousChange)
- Keyboard shortcuts within the diff view: Cmd+Down (next change), Cmd+Up (previous change)

## Why

Same rationale as D3 (editor bridge): the diff viewer is AppKit but needs to live in the SwiftUI layout. The proxy enables the inspector panel or toolbar to drive navigation ("jump to next change" button).

## Considerations

- The diff computation should happen on a background thread (it can be slow for large documents) and results should be delivered to the main thread for rendering.
- updateNSView should only recompute the diff if the input strings actually changed (compare with stored snapshots).
- The keyboard shortcuts should be active when the diff view is focused.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Diff view renders correctly inside SwiftUI layout
- [ ] Input changes trigger diff recomputation
- [ ] Proxy enables programmatic change navigation
- [ ] Keyboard shortcuts work for next/previous change
close_reason: done

title: Navigation router + phase-based content switching
description:
## What

Implement a navigation router that maps sidebar selections to content views:
- Enum-based route model: .planEditor(planId), .planGeneration, .planWizard, .multiModelSynthesis(planId), .refinement(planId), .versionHistory(planId)
- @Observable NavigationRouter class that drives ContentArea's view switching
- Programmatic navigation support (e.g., after plan creation, navigate to editor)
- Back/forward navigation via toolbar buttons (breadcrumb-style for nested views)

## Why

Phase 0 has ~6 distinct content views. Without a proper router, the content switching logic will be a tangled mess of @State booleans. A clean router also sets the pattern for Phase 1+ when we add bead views, swarm dashboard, etc. Building it right now saves a painful refactor later.

## Considerations

- The router should be @Observable (not ObservableObject) since we're on Swift 6.
- Store the route stack as an array for back/forward support.
- The router does NOT own any data — it only tracks which view is active and with what parameters.
- Consider making the route enum Codable for state restoration across app launches.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Sidebar selections navigate to the correct content view
- [ ] Programmatic navigation works (e.g., wizard completion → editor)
- [ ] Back navigation returns to the previous view
- [ ] Route state persists across window size changes (no view reset)
