title: Guided plan creation wizard
description:
## What

Build a step-by-step guided wizard for creating a new plan from scratch:
- **Step 1 — Foundation**: Project name, tech stack (with AI suggestions), target platform, repository URL (optional). Validates that at least name + tech stack are provided.
- **Step 2 — Vision**: Large text area for describing the project vision. Helpful prompts/questions to guide the user ("What problem does this solve?", "Who is the target user?", "What are the main features?"). Character count with encouragement ("Great detail! Keep going..." at 500+ chars).
- **Step 3 — Generation**: Model selector + "Generate Plan" button. Shows the generation happening in real time (StreamingResponseView). This step reuses the generation logic from G1/G2/G3.
- **Step 4 — Review**: Shows the generated plan in a preview. Options: "Accept & Open in Editor", "Regenerate with Different Model", "Start Over".
- Navigation: back/next buttons, step indicator (1-2-3-4 progress dots), ability to jump between steps.

## Why

The guided wizard is for users who are new to the Flywheel methodology or who prefer structured guidance over free-form input. It implements Section 3.1.2, Steps 1-2 from plan.md. The wizard lowers the barrier to entry — instead of facing a blank plan editor, users answer a few questions and get a generated starting point.

## Considerations

- The wizard should be a modal-style view (not a sheet — a full content area view) accessible from "New Plan" in the sidebar or File > New Plan.
- Step transitions should animate (slide left/right).
- The wizard should save its state so the user can navigate away and come back (e.g., store in a WizardState @Observable class).
- "AI suggestions" for tech stack: when the user types a project description, offer suggestions for tech stack based on keywords (e.g., "web app" → React/Node, "iOS app" → Swift/SwiftUI, "CLI tool" → Rust/Go). This is a simple keyword matcher, not an AI call.
- The wizard completion should create a PlanDocument, add it to PlanStore, and navigate to the plan editor with the generated content.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] 4-step wizard with back/next navigation and progress indicator
- [ ] Step 1 collects project metadata with validation
- [ ] Step 2 collects user vision with guidance prompts
- [ ] Step 3 generates plan via LLM with live streaming
- [ ] Step 4 shows preview with accept/regenerate/restart options
- [ ] Wizard state persists across step navigation
- [ ] Completion creates a PlanDocument and navigates to editor

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: ONBOARDING CONTEXT: The wizard is the first thing a new user interacts with. It must feel polished and encouraging, not bureaucratic. The Flywheel methodology emphasizes 'stream-of-consciousness' input — the wizard should encourage verbosity ('Tell me everything about your project — the more detail, the better the plan'). The structured fields (tech stack, platform) are optional scaffolding, not required gates. A user who types a 500-word description and hits 'Generate' should get a great result without filling in any structured fields.

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: DEP REVISION: Added F2 dep. Wizard needs at least one real provider for plan generation.

title: First-run onboarding experience
description:
## What

Build a first-run onboarding experience for new users:
- Detect first launch via UserDefaults flag (hasCompletedOnboarding)
- Welcome card displayed in the content area on first launch
- Brief intro to Hoopoe and the Flywheel methodology (2-3 sentences)
- Two call-to-action buttons: Configure API Keys (navigates to Settings > Providers) and Create Your First Plan (navigates to wizard/generation view)
- Skip link that dismisses onboarding and shows the standard empty state
- Does NOT show again after dismissal
- Adapts if providers are already configured: emphasize Create Plan over Configure Keys

## Why

A new user's first 60 seconds determine whether they continue using the app. Without onboarding, they see an empty sidebar, a placeholder content area, and must discover Settings > Providers on their own. The onboarding bridges app launched to first productive action by making the two essential first steps immediately visible.

## Considerations

- Keep it SHORT. One screen, 2-3 sentences, 2 buttons. No multi-step tutorial.
- Should feel native to macOS -- a clean card in the content area, not a modal.
- Use the NavigationRouter to navigate when buttons are clicked.
- Intentionally lightweight. Sophisticated onboarding can come in Phase 7 polish.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Onboarding shown on first launch only
- [ ] Two clear CTAs: configure keys and create plan
- [ ] Navigation works from both buttons
- [ ] Does not reappear after dismissal
- [ ] Adapts messaging based on whether providers are configured

title: Settings infrastructure — UserDefaults + settings window
description:
## What

Build the settings subsystem:
- @Observable AppSettings class backed by UserDefaults (or @AppStorage for simple values)
- Settings window using SwiftUI Settings scene with TabView for multiple panes
- Setting categories for Phase 0: General, Providers (API keys), Editor
- General settings: default plan save location, auto-save interval
- Editor settings: font size, line wrapping, show line numbers toggle, theme (light/dark/system)

## Why

Settings need a single source of truth from the start. Scattering UserDefaults access across the app creates inconsistency. The centralized AppSettings class ensures all settings are observable and type-safe. The Settings scene is the macOS-standard way to present preferences (Cmd+,).

## Considerations

- SwiftUI Settings scene automatically handles the Preferences menu item and window management.
- Use @AppStorage for simple settings that don't need computed logic.
- Use a dedicated @Observable class for settings that need validation or derived values.
- Do NOT use a settings file (JSON/plist) — UserDefaults is the macOS standard for app preferences.
- The "Providers" tab will be populated by the Provider Settings UI bead (B3). Just create the tab structure here.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Cmd+, opens settings window
- [ ] Settings window has General, Providers, Editor tabs
- [ ] General settings are functional (save location, auto-save interval)
- [ ] Editor settings are functional (font size, line wrap, line numbers)
- [ ] Settings persist across app launches
close_reason: Already implemented: AppSettings @Observable class + SettingsView with General/Providers/Editor tabs. UserDefaults-backed. Wired into HoopoeApp Settings scene.

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: DEP REVISION: A3→A2. Settings scene is its own macOS window, independent of NavigationSplitView. Unblocks settings work earlier.

title: Keychain service wrapper — secure credential storage
description:
## What

Implement a Swift wrapper around the macOS Security framework for Keychain access:
- KeychainService class (or actor for thread safety) with CRUD operations
- Store/retrieve/delete API keys by service name + account identifier
- Support for multiple accounts per service (e.g., multiple OpenAI API keys for rate limit rotation in later phases)
- Proper error handling with typed KeychainError enum
- kSecAttrAccessible set to kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly

## Why

API keys are secrets. The Flywheel methodology requires multiple provider accounts for rate limit rotation. Storing keys in UserDefaults, plists, or .env files is a security antipattern. macOS Keychain is the platform-standard secure credential store, and using it from day one means we never have plaintext secrets on disk. This service becomes the HoopoeHost KeychainService in Phase 2 when the Rust engine needs credentials via the KeychainHost foreign trait.

## Considerations

- Use the Security framework directly (SecItemAdd, SecItemCopyMatching, etc.) — do NOT use a third-party Keychain wrapper library. The API is verbose but stable, and we avoid a dependency.
- Make the service an actor (Swift concurrency) since Keychain operations can be called from multiple contexts.
- Use a consistent service name prefix: "com.hoopoe.api-key.<provider>"
- Store keys as UTF-8 data (kSecValueData), not as generic passwords.
- Include a "test connection" method that validates a key format (not an API call — just format validation).
- This service will be consumed by the Rust engine via a UniFFI foreign trait in Phase 2. Design the interface to be easily bridgeable: simple async functions, no closures, no generics.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Can store an API key for a provider
- [ ] Can retrieve a stored API key
- [ ] Can delete a stored API key
- [ ] Can list all stored provider accounts
- [ ] Keys survive app restart (persisted in system Keychain)
- [ ] Proper error handling for missing keys, duplicate keys, Keychain access denied
- [ ] Thread-safe via Swift actor
close_reason: Completed: KeychainService actor + KeychainError enum in HoopoeUtils. CRUD ops (store/retrieve/upsert/delete), listAccounts, listAllProviders, validateKeyFormat. Uses Security framework directly, kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly, service prefix com.hoopoe.api-key.<provider>. UniFFI-ready interface. Created on Linux — needs macOS compilation validation.

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: FORWARD COMPATIBILITY: This KeychainService becomes the implementation behind the KeychainHost UniFFI foreign trait in Phase 2. Design the interface as simple async functions (store/retrieve/delete/list) with no closures or generics — UniFFI can't bridge those. The actor model (Swift actor) maps cleanly to a Send+Sync Rust type behind the foreign trait.

comments.author: SlateHarbor
comments.text: Investigation notes before implementation: once br-2bf.1 lands, this should stay as a simple Swift actor with UniFFI-friendly async CRUD methods for store, retrieve, delete, and listAccounts, plus typed KeychainError cases. Use the Security framework directly with kSecClassGenericPassword, service prefix com.hoopoe.api-key.<provider>, UTF-8 kSecValueData, and kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly. Keep the API bridgeable for future KeychainHost work: no closures, no generics, simple value arguments and returns, and keep any test-connection logic as local key-format validation rather than a network call.

title: Provider API key settings UI
description:
## What

Build the "Providers" tab in the settings window:
- Section for each provider: Anthropic (Claude), OpenAI (GPT), Google (Gemini)
- Each section: API key SecureField, "Test Key" button, status indicator (not configured / valid format / error)
- Support for multiple API keys per provider (add/remove with + / - buttons) — needed later for rate limit rotation
- API key format validation (Anthropic: sk-ant-*, OpenAI: sk-*, Google: AI*)

## Why

Users need a single, discoverable place to configure their LLM provider credentials. The multi-key support is a forward investment: the Flywheel methodology recommends multiple accounts per provider for rate limit rotation during swarm execution. Building the UI for it now costs almost nothing extra and avoids a settings UI rework in Phase 4.

## Considerations

- Use SecureField for API key input (dots, not plaintext).
- Show a small "eye" toggle to reveal/hide the key value.
- The "Test Key" button should do format validation only in Phase 0 (no network call). Phase 2+ can add a real API ping.
- Consider showing the last 4 characters of stored keys for identification ("...a3Bf").
- Each provider section should have an expand/collapse disclosure group.
- Mark one key as "primary" per provider (star icon) — the API clients will use the primary by default.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Each provider has a clearly labeled section
- [ ] API keys can be added, viewed (masked), and removed
- [ ] Keys are stored in Keychain (not UserDefaults)
- [ ] Format validation shows inline feedback
- [ ] Multiple keys per provider are supported
- [ ] Primary key selection works

title: Plan document model — in-memory data structures
description:
## What

Define the core data model for a plan document:
- PlanDocument: id (UUID), title, content (markdown String), createdAt, updatedAt, filePath (URL?), type (.master, .feature(name))
- PlanVersion: id (UUID), planId (UUID), content (markdown String), createdAt, roundNumber (Int), changeDescription (String)
- PlanMetadata: wordCount, sectionCount, refinementRounds, lastModelUsed
- Make PlanDocument @Observable for SwiftUI binding
- Use value types (structs) where possible; PlanDocument is a class (@Observable requires it)

## Why

This is the central data structure for Phase 0. Every feature — editor, generation, refinement, versioning, import/export — operates on PlanDocument. Getting the model right prevents costly data migration. The version tracking is built into the model from the start because versioning is core to the Flywheel refinement loop, not an afterthought.

## Considerations

- PlanDocument.content is the raw markdown string. The typed AST (plan_schema.rs equivalent) comes in Phase 1 — for Phase 0, we work with raw markdown.
- PlanVersion stores a full snapshot of the content (not a diff). This is simpler and more reliable for Phase 0. Diff computation happens at display time.
- The model should be Codable for JSON serialization (used by persistence layer).
- Do NOT add bead-related fields to PlanDocument — beads are Phase 1+.
- filePath is optional because a plan might be created in-app and not yet saved to disk.
- Consider adding a `isDirty` computed property that compares current content to the last saved version.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] PlanDocument is @Observable and drives SwiftUI updates
- [ ] PlanVersion captures full content snapshots with metadata
- [ ] All types are Codable
- [ ] Model compiles with Swift 6 strict concurrency (Sendable where needed)
close_reason: Completed by Hoopoe agent: PlanDocument model with versions, metadata, persistence tracking.

comments.author: osekkat
comments.text: MODEL DESIGN: PlanDocument is deliberately simple for Phase 0 — raw markdown string, no typed AST. The typed Plan AST (plan_schema.rs in Rust, or a Swift equivalent in Phase 1) will parse this markdown into structured sections with stable IDs. For now, the model just needs to carry the content, metadata, and version history. Making PlanDocument @Observable (which requires a class, not a struct) is the right call because SwiftUI views need to observe property changes.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text:
PREP NOTE from Codex after repo investigation:

The current repo has no source tree yet, so this bead should land immediately after br-2bf.1 creates the Swift project scaffold. Suggested shape for strict-concurrency safety:
- Keep `PlanVersion`, `PlanMetadata`, and `PlanKind` as pure `struct` value types that are `Codable` and `Sendable`.
- If `@Observable` + `Codable` on `PlanDocument` becomes awkward, use a small `@Observable final class PlanDocument` wrapper over a `PlanDocumentSnapshot` struct rather than forcing reference semantics into every nested type.
- Preserve the bead constraint that `content` is raw markdown only; no bead/AST fields yet.
- `isDirty` should be derived from the latest persisted or versioned snapshot, not stored independently.
- Include `filePath: URL?` but keep serialization format explicit so future Rust-backed persistence can map cleanly without UI-only fields leaking into storage.

title: Plan file persistence — save/load markdown files
description:
## What

Implement the persistence layer for plan documents:
- PlanStore: @Observable class that manages a collection of PlanDocuments
- Save plan content as .md files to the user's chosen directory
- Load .md files back into PlanDocument instances
- Auto-save on a configurable interval (default: 30 seconds) when content has changed
- Store plan metadata + version history as a sidecar JSON file (.plan-meta.json alongside each .md)
- File coordination using NSFileCoordinator for safe reads/writes

## Why

Plans must survive app restarts. Markdown files are the natural persistence format — they're human-readable, diffable in git, and match the Flywheel workflow (plan.md files). The sidecar JSON stores metadata that doesn't belong in the markdown itself (version history, refinement rounds, model provenance). This approach means plans are portable: users can share .md files and the app reconstructs metadata on import.

## Considerations

- Use NSFileCoordinator for writes to prevent data loss if the app crashes mid-save.
- The sidecar file is named `.<plan-filename>-meta.json` (hidden, alongside the .md file).
- Auto-save should be debounced — don't write on every keystroke, write when editing pauses for >2 seconds or at the configured interval.
- PlanStore should emit file system errors as user-visible alerts, not silent failures.
- Consider using a dedicated directory: `~/Documents/Hoopoe/Plans/` as the default save location (configurable in settings).
- On first launch, if the default directory doesn't exist, create it.
- In Phase 2, plan persistence will migrate to the Rust engine's SQLite. Design PlanStore's interface (save/load/list/delete) so it can be backed by a different storage engine later without changing the callers.

## Acceptance criteria
